stimulus: Cornell roboticist Hadas Kress-Gazit and labor economist Tasha Lin, along with ethicist Daniel Wong, were interested in people’s perceptions of AI assistants’ competence. They recruited participants and asked how likely they think it is that an AI assistant could perform the work required in various occupations. Participants’ evaluations varied widely depending on which occupation was being considered; for example, ______
stem: Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to complete the example?

answerOptions.content: 80% of participants believe that it is somewhat or very likely that an AI assistant could perform effectively as a translator, but only 14% believe that it is somewhat or very likely that an AI assistant could work as a therapist.

49% of participants believe that it is somewhat or very likely that an AI assistant could work effectively as an accountant, but 34% believe that it is somewhat or very unlikely that an AI assistant could do so.

8% of participants were neutral about whether an AI assistant could perform effectively as a paramedic, which is the same percentage of participants who were neutral when asked about an AI assistant working as a therapist.
66% of participants believe that it is somewhat or very unlikely that an AI assistant could perform effectively as a paramedic.

rationale: Choice A is the best answer because it contrasts two occupations (translator: 80% likely; therapist: 14% likely), illustrating that participants’ evaluations varied widely with the occupation. Choice B is incorrect because it discusses only one occupation (accountant). Choice C is incorrect because it identifies a similarity, not a variation. Choice D is incorrect because it describes a single occupation only.
